worth_it_explanation: Console access is the layer where a publisher controls who can change pricing, deals, and inventory settings inside TripleLift, so loose access turns every other monetization control into something a wide group of people can quietly change. The review reads the user list, the role assigned to each person, and how API credentials are issued and rotated, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time. The finding points to a concrete access decision the publisher can act on, such as removing a departed contractor or narrowing an over-broad admin role.
intro: Access governance decides who can see and change a publisher's setup inside the TripleLift console and through its application programming interface (API). The role each person holds, whether departed staff still have a login, and how API credentials are scoped and rotated all sit underneath the pricing, deal, and inventory work the rest of the audit covers. This subcategory reviews the access layer itself, the people and credentials that can reach the account, rather than the monetization settings they operate on. These checks confirm that access follows least privilege, that the user list reflects who actually works on the account today, and that machine credentials are scoped to a purpose and rotated on a schedule.

audit_checks.title: Console roles are broader than each person's work requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When most users hold an admin-level role, a mistake or a compromised login can change pricing, deals, or inventory settings across the whole account rather than one corner of it. Broad access widens the blast radius of every error and every breach, so the gap matters even when nothing has gone wrong yet.

educate.base:
The principle of least privilege holds that each person should have the minimum access needed to do their job, and nothing more. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) states the idea plainly, that a system should restrict access privileges to the minimum necessary to accomplish assigned tasks. Inside an advertising console, that means a person who reviews reports does not need the role that lets them rewrite pricing rules, and a person who manages one set of deals does not need account-wide administrative control.

TripleLift, like most supply-side platforms, separates console access into roles that carry different powers. The reason least privilege matters here is the blast radius. An admin role can change pricing, deals, inventory configuration, and the user list itself. When that role is handed to everyone who touches the account, a single mistake or a single compromised password can change settings far beyond the one area the person actually works in. Narrowing each person to the role their work requires keeps an error or a breach contained to that person's corner of the account.

The review is a read of the user list against what each person does. For each user, the audit pairs the role they hold with the work they actually perform, then asks whether the role is wider than the work. A reporting analyst sitting in an admin role, or a former agency contact who still holds full access, are the kinds of gaps this surfaces.

A few signs point to roles that are broader than the work requires:
  • Most or all users hold an admin-level role regardless of what they do on the account
  • People who only read reports or manage a single deal set hold account-wide change permissions
  • The publisher cannot explain why a given person needs the role they hold
You may want to confirm that each person's role matches the work they do. One approach is to walk the user list with the account owner and step down any role that is wider than the person's actual responsibilities.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media business, ad operations, sales, and finance often touch the same console for different reasons, and it can be easier to grant everyone admin than to map roles to teams. You may want to set the reporting-only people to a read role and reserve change permissions for the ad operations staff who actually configure pricing and deals.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business, the team monetizing editorial inventory is usually small, and a single shared admin login can stand in for individual accounts. You may want to give each person their own login at the narrowest role their work needs, so access can be traced to a person and removed cleanly when someone leaves.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed documentation on console roles and the permissions each one carries is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the console user list and pair the role each person holds with the work they actually do, looking for roles that are wider than the responsibility.

console_user_admin.label: TripleLift console user management
steps: Open the user or account management area of the TripleLift console and list every active user with the role assigned to each.
For each user, record their actual responsibility on the account, for example reporting only, deal management, or full administration.
Compare the role to the responsibility and flag any user whose role grants more than their work requires, such as a reporting analyst holding an admin role.
Note how many users hold an admin-level role and whether that count is larger than the number of people who genuinely administer the account.

access_inventory.label: Access inventory worksheet
steps: Build a simple list of every person with access, their role, their team, and the reason they need that level of access.
Walk the list with the account owner and mark each row as justified or over-broad.
Record the over-broad rows as the finding, with the role each holds and the narrower role their work would support.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each user, the role they hold, and the work they do. A user list where every role matches the holder's responsibility, with admin reserved for the people who genuinely administer the account, passes. A list where reporting-only or single-purpose users hold account-wide change permissions, or where the admin count is larger than the number of actual administrators, is a fail recorded with the users and roles affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user, the role they hold, and the work they do above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several users in {{site_name}}'s TripleLift console hold roles broader than their work requires, with account-wide change permissions granted to people whose responsibility is narrower. The principle of least privilege holds that each person should have the minimum access needed for their task, and broad roles widen the blast radius of any mistake or compromised login across the whole account rather than one corner of it. {{voice_recommend}} walking the user list with the account owner, pairing each person's role with the work they actually do, and stepping down any role that is wider than the responsibility. After the change, reserve admin access for the people who genuinely administer the account.

audit_checks.title: Departed staff and former partners still hold active logins
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An active login for someone who has left the business is access that no current employee is watching, which is the kind of credential an attacker reuses and a former insider can still act through. The login keeps its powers until someone removes it, so a stale account is a standing entry point rather than a tidiness problem.

educate.base:
A login outlives the person it was created for unless someone removes it. When an employee changes teams, a contractor's engagement ends, or an agency relationship closes, the console account they used keeps working with all the powers it had on the day they left. Nobody is monitoring that account anymore, which is exactly what makes it a soft target. A former insider can still act through it, and a password that leaks in an unrelated breach becomes a working key to the publisher's monetization setup.

This is the access side of the same least-privilege idea. Least privilege limits how much each active person can do. Removing departed users limits who counts as an active person at all. The two work together, so a clean role map means little if the user list still carries people who no longer work on the account. NIST's guidance on least privilege includes reviewing access on a schedule and removing privileges that are no longer needed, which is precisely what a stale login fails.

The review compares the console user list against the people who currently work on the account. Any login that maps to someone who has left, whether a former employee, a rolled-off contractor, or a closed agency contact, is the finding. The check also looks at whether the publisher has a routine that removes access when someone departs, rather than relying on noticing a stale account during an audit.

A few signs point to departed users still holding access:
  • The user list includes names the publisher does not recognize as current staff or partners
  • Former contractors or agency contacts appear with active logins after their engagement ended
  • There is no offboarding step that removes console access when a person leaves
You may want to confirm that every active login maps to a current person who needs it. One approach is to reconcile the user list against a current staff and partner roster, then build an offboarding step that removes console access the day someone departs.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media business, agency partners and freelance ad operations help often come and go, and their console logins can outlast the engagement. You may want to reconcile the user list against current partner relationships and remove any login tied to an agency or freelancer who is no longer engaged.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business, monetization is sometimes handled by a small team or an outside consultant whose access was never removed when the project ended. You may want to confirm that any consultant login is removed at the close of the engagement rather than left active for a future that may not arrive.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's documentation on adding and removing console users and managing their access is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the console user list against the people who currently work on the account and flag any login that maps to a departed employee, contractor, or partner.

console_user_admin.label: TripleLift console user management
steps: Open the user or account management area of the TripleLift console and export or list every active user.
Reconcile the list against a current roster of staff, contractors, and agency partners who work on the account.
Flag any login that does not map to a current person, recording who it belonged to and when that person left.
Ask the publisher whether an offboarding routine removes console access when someone departs, and record whether one exists.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner reconciles the console user list against a current roster of staff and partners. A user list where every active login maps to a current person who needs it, supported by an offboarding step that removes access on departure, passes. A list carrying logins for departed employees, rolled-off contractors, or closed agency contacts is a fail recorded with each stale login and the person it belonged to.
evaluate.fallback_message: Reconcile the console user list against a current staff and partner roster above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s TripleLift console still carries active logins for people who no longer work on the account, including former staff or partners. A login keeps all of its powers until someone removes it, so a stale account is a standing entry point that no current employee is watching, and it is the kind of credential reused after an unrelated password leak. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the user list against a current staff and partner roster, removing every login that does not map to a current person, and building an offboarding step that removes console access the day someone departs. After the cleanup, every active login should map to a current person who needs it.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared, broadly scoped, or never rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An API credential is a key that runs without a person behind it, so a shared or broadly scoped token that never expires is a long-lived secret many people can use and an attacker can keep using once it leaks. Scoping and rotation limit both how much a leaked credential can do and how long it stays valid, so a credential left wide and static is a durable exposure.

educate.base:
Beyond the console, a publisher often reaches TripleLift through an application programming interface (API) or a Prebid Server integration, both of which run on machine credentials rather than a person logging in. A credential of this kind is a key that acts on its own, which makes its scope and lifetime the whole of its security. The same least-privilege idea applies, so a credential should carry only the access the integration needs and should not double as a general-purpose key.

Two habits weaken machine credentials. The first is sharing, where one token is pasted into several systems or handed around a team, so no one can say which system or person a given call came from and a single leak exposes everything that token can reach. The second is a credential that never expires, which stays valid long after the person who created it has moved on and long after it may have leaked. Rotating credentials on a schedule limits how long any one secret is useful, and scoping them to a purpose limits how much a leaked secret can do.

Prebid's own guidance reflects this discipline. Its documentation for building a server-side adapter notes that account-specific values such as an application token are passed through defined configuration, and that Prebid Server is not an open proxy, so credentials and endpoints are constrained by design rather than left open. The audit question for the publisher is narrower, namely whether the credentials they hold for TripleLift are issued per integration, scoped to a purpose, and rotated, or whether one broad token is shared and left in place indefinitely.

A few signs point to API credentials that are unscoped or unrotated:
  • One API credential or token is reused across multiple systems or shared among team members
  • Credentials carry broad access rather than being scoped to the specific integration that uses them
  • No rotation schedule exists, so tokens stay valid indefinitely with no record of when they were last changed
You may want to confirm that each integration holds its own scoped credential and that a rotation schedule is in place. One approach is to issue a separate credential per integration, document what each is for, and set a recurring date to rotate them.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media business, a reporting pipeline, a Prebid Server setup, and an internal dashboard may each call the same API, and it can be tempting to share one token across all three. You may want to issue a separate credential for each integration so a leak in one pipeline does not hand an attacker access to the others.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business, an API credential is sometimes created once for a launch and then left untouched for years. You may want to set a rotation schedule and record when each credential was last changed, so a token does not stay valid indefinitely after the person who created it has moved on.

learn_more.source_title: Building a Bid Adapter (Go): account parameters and endpoint constraints

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's API reference and credential management documentation are in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review how API credentials and access tokens for TripleLift are issued, scoped, and rotated, looking for shared tokens, broad access, or credentials that never expire.

credential_inventory.label: Credential inventory worksheet
steps: List every API credential or access token the publisher holds for TripleLift, including any used in a Prebid Server integration.
For each credential, record which system or integration uses it, what access it carries, and whether it is shared across systems or people.
Record when each credential was created and last rotated, and whether any rotation schedule exists.
Flag any credential that is shared, broadly scoped beyond its integration, or has no record of rotation.

console_api_settings.label: TripleLift console API or token settings
steps: Open the area of the TripleLift console where API access or tokens are managed, if available to the publisher's role.
Note how many credentials exist, what each is scoped to, and whether the console shows a last-used or last-rotated date.
Record any credential that appears unused, over-scoped, or long-lived as a candidate to rotate or revoke.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each API credential, the integration that uses it, its scope, and its rotation history. A setup where each integration holds its own scoped credential and a rotation schedule is in place passes. A setup with a shared token, credentials scoped more broadly than the integration needs, or tokens that have never been rotated is a fail recorded with each credential and the weakness it shows.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each API credential, the integration that uses it, its scope, and its rotation history above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s API credentials for TripleLift are shared across systems, scoped more broadly than the integration needs, or never rotated. An API credential is a key that runs without a person behind it, so a shared or static token is a long-lived secret many people can use and an attacker can keep using once it leaks. {{voice_recommend}} issuing a separate credential per integration, scoping each to the access its integration needs, and setting a recurring date to rotate them with a record of when each was last changed. After the change, each integration should hold its own scoped credential on a rotation schedule rather than one broad token left in place indefinitely.
